Double-headed snake logic: Squares 40, 47, 54, 63, 72, and 89 all have isDoubleHead: true. Pairs (40,47), (54,63), and (72,89) share a tail. Your Week 2 move handler will use this flag to apply the correct snakeTo destination.

5. Run and verify

cds watch

Open http://localhost:4004. You should see GameService with endpoints for GameSessions, Players, and BoardSquares.

Test the board data is loaded:

GET http://localhost:4004/odata/v4/GameService/BoardSquares?$filter=snakeTo gt 0

You should see all 9 snake-head squares returned.

How to Submit

Reply to the Week 1 blog post on SAP Community with a comment containing:

  1. Your db/schema.cds — paste as a CDS code block
  2. Your srv/game-service.cds — paste as a CDS code block
  3. A screenshot of http://localhost:4004 showing the GameService endpoints
  4. One sentence explaining your choice of TurnStatus enum values — did you add any of your own?

What's Next

In Week 2 — Roll & Move, you implement the bound actions. You'll write the rollDice handler, apply snake and ladder logic, and see CAP automatically enforce the @from / @TO transitions — returning HTTP 409 when a player tries to roll out of turn.

Resources

    • CAP Status Flows Documentation
    • CAP Node.js Documentation
    • Initial Setup 
    • cds-cli : cds add http

entry.title: Trigger an SAP Build Process Automation Workflow from CAP Using the @cap-js/process Plugin
entry.content:
Overview

 

Previously, triggering an SAP Build Process Automation (SBPA) workflow from a CAP application required direct REST API calls. On April 26, 2026, the Node.js plugin `@cap-js/process` was released as its first beta, offering a native CAP integration. This guide walks through the complete setup step by step.

 

Prerequisites
  • Your BTP subaccount is set up for SAP Build Process Automation. Refer to the SBPA initial setup guide
  • CAP local development tools are installed. Refer to CAP initial setup .
Procedure

 

Step 1 — Develop and Deploy a Process in SBPA
Create and deploy your process in the SAP Build Process Automation designer.
 

 
After deployment, locate the workflow instance ID in the deployment details.
 

 

In this example the process definition ID is:
ap10.iac-apj-workshop-i30jhz3m.wfcap1.wfcap1
 
Note this value — it is required in Steps 5 and 7.

 

Step 2 — Generate a Sample CAP Project
cds init capwf --nodejs --add sample
cd capwf
 

 

 

Step 3 — Add the `@cap-js/process` Plugin
npm add @cap-js/process
 

 

 

Step 4 — Bind the CAP Project to the SBPA Service Instance

 

Log in to Cloud Foundry and bind the `ProcessService` to your SBPA service instance.
cf login -a https://api.cf.<cf-data-center>.hana.ondemand.com --sso
cds bind ProcessService -2 <service-instance>

 

> Replace `<cf-data-center>` with your Cloud Foundry region (e.g. `eu10`, `us10`) and `<service-instance>` with the name of your SBPA service instance.

 

 

Step 5 — Import the Process as a CDS Service

 

Use the `process_definition_id` from [Step 1].
cds import --from process --name <process_definition_id>
 

 

 

Step 6 — Open the Project in VS Code

 

 

 

Step 7 — Annotate the CDS Service to Trigger the Workflow

 

Edit `srv/admin-service.cds` to add `@bpm.process` annotations to the `Books` entity.

 

Before:
using {sap.capire.bookshop as my} from '../db/schema';
service AdminService {
entity Authors as projection on my.Authors;
entity Books as projection on my.Books;
entity Genres as projection on my.Genres;
}
 

 

After:
using {sap.capire.bookshop as my} from '../db/schema';
service AdminService {
  entity Authors as projection on my.Authors;
  @bpm.process.start           : {
    id    : 'ap10.iac-apj-workshop-i30jhz3m.wfcap1.wfcap1',
    on    : 'submit',
    inputs: [
      { path: $self.idStr,        as: 'id'       },
      { path: $self.authorName,   as: 'author'   },
      $self.title,
      $self.descr,
      { path: $self.genreName,    as: 'genre'    },
      $self.stock,
      $self.price,
      { path: $self.currencyCode, as: 'currency' }
    ]
  }
  @bpm.process.cancel #one     : {on: 'DELETE'}
  @bpm.process.businessKey #one: (title)
  @bpm.process.businessKey     : (ID)
  entity Books   as projection on my.Books {
    *,
    cast(ID as String)    as idStr       : String,
    author.name           as authorName  : String,
    genre.name            as genreName   : String,
    currency.code         as currencyCode: String
  }
  actions {
    action submit();
  };
  entity Genres  as projection on my.Genres;
}
Key annotations explained:
AnnotationPurpose
@BPM.process.startTriggers the SBPA workflow when the `submit` action is called, mapping entity fields to process inputs
@BPM.process.cancelCancels the running workflow instance when the entity is deleted
@BPM.process.businessKeySets the business key used to correlate the workflow instance with the CAP entity
 

 

Step 8 — Update file srv/admin-service.js to add the following code.

    this.on('submit', Books, async (req) => {
      // console.log(req)
      return req.subject
    })

 

 
 
Step 9 — Run the Application Locally

 

Start the application in hybrid mode so it connects to the bound SBPA service instance.
cds watch --profile hybrid
 

 

 

Step 10 — Test the `submit` Action

 

Create `test/http/AdminService.http` and send the following request to trigger the SBPA workflow for an existing active book.

 

@server=http://localhost:4004
@username=alice
@password=

### Books Submit action on an existing active Book (ID=201)
# @NAME Books_Submit_Existing
POST {{server}}/odata/v4/admin/Books(ID=201,IsActiveEntity=true)/AdminService.submit
Content-Type: application/json
Authorization: Basic {{username}}:{{password}}


{}

 

 

 

The terminal confirms the workflow was triggered — notice `[process] – Starting process ap10.iac-apj-workshop-i30jhz3m.wfcap1.wfcap1` and the book data passed as inputs.

 

 

Step 11 — Verify the Workflow Task in SBPA My Inbox

 

Open the SAP Build Process Automation **My Inbox**. The approval task appears with the book data (ID, author, title, description, genre, stock, price, currency) populated from the CAP entity.

 

 
The End!
 
Thank you for your time!

entry.title: The Easiest Way to Expose a CAP Node.js Project as an MCP Server
entry.content:
Overview
 
The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard that allows AI agents and tools to connect to external data sources and services. With the `@cap-js/mcp` package, you can expose a CAP Node.js application as an MCP server in just a few steps — no manual REST wiring required.
This guide walks through the complete setup from a fresh CAP project to a running MCP server you can inspect with the MCP Inspector.
 
Prerequisites
  • CAP local development tools installed — see CAP Initial Setup
  • Node.js and npm available on your machine
Procedure
 
Step 1 — Generate a Sample CAP Project
cds init capmcp --nodejs --add sample
cd capmcp

 

 

Step 2 — Add the `@cap-js/mcp` Plugin
npm add @cap-js/mcp

 

 

Step 3 — Annotate the Service with `@mcp`
 
Open `srv/cat-service.cds` and add the `@mcp` annotation to the service you want to expose:
annotate CatalogService with @McP:'books';
 

 

 

Step 4 — Add Doc Comments to the `Authors` Entity
 
Open `db/schema.cds` and add JSDoc-style comments to the `Authors` entity. These comments are surfaced as tool descriptions to the AI agent.
/**
 * This is the author entity.
 * It contains information about book authors.
 */
entity Authors {
  /** The ID of the author. */
  ID : Integer;
  /** The name of the author. */
  name : String;
  /** The books written by the author. */
  books : Association to many Books;
}
 
Step 5 — Configure a Mock User for MCP
 
Open `package.json` and add the `mcp` section under `cds` to configure authentication for the MCP endpoint:
"cds": {
  "mcp": {
    "autowire": {
      "user": "admin",
      "password": "admin"
    }
  }
}

 
Step 6 — Run the Application
 
Start the CAP server locally:
cds watch

 
Step 7 — Inspect the MCP Tools
 
Launch the MCP Inspector to verify the tools your server exposes:
npx @modelcontextprotocol/inspector
 
 

 

Connect to `http://localhost:4004/mcp` and set the Authorization header to:
Basic YWRtaW46YWRtaW4=
 
This is the Base64-encoded value of `admin:admin`, matching the credentials configured in Step 5.
 
 
 
 

 

The End!
 
Thank you for your time!

entry.title: Code Connect 2026: Final Info Before We Kick Off!
entry.content:
Code Connect 2026 is just around the corner! From July 13–16, 2026, the SAP developer community will gather in St. Leon-Rot, Germany – onsite and online.

Here’s everything you need to know before we get started.

 

 

We Are Fully Booked!

All onsite tickets are fully booked - thank you for the amazing interest!

If you couldn’t secure a ticket:

  • Join the waiting lists on Code Connect 2026 in case seats become available.
  • Follow selected sessions via live stream or Microsoft Teams link.

Even if you're not onsite, you can still be part of Code Connect. Selected sessions will be streamed live, and recordings of some sessions will be available afterwards. Check back soon for details.

 

 

 

UI5con (July 14)

  • Main stage sessions will be live streamed on YouTube.
  • Check the agenda to see which sessions are streamed.

 

 

re>≡CAP (July 15)

  • Main stage sessions will be broadcasted.
  • Some sidetracks (W1/W2) can be attended via Microsoft Teams link.
  • Check the agenda to see which sessions are broadcasted or available via Microsoft Teams link.

 

 

HANA Tech Con (July 16)

  • The links will be announced on the event day.

 

The Agendas Are Live

The full program is already available - time to plan your schedule!

Check-In opens at 8.00 AM every day. Please bring the QR code with you that you can find in your ticket.

July 13 – Code Jams & Community Meetup

  • Hands-on Code Jams:
    • OpenUI5
    • CAP
    • AI Agents
  • Informal pre-event meetup (no registration needed):
    Ihle Besen, Höfe am Sträßel 3, 69231 Rauenberg
    5:00 PM CEST

July 14–16 – Main Conference Days

Explore all agendas:

  • July 14: UI5con
  • July 15: re>≡CAP
  • July 16: HANA Tech Con

On July 14 also the HANA AI CodeJam takes place.

 

Location

Code Connect takes place at:

SAP-Allee 27
68789 St. Leon-Rot, Germany

Find detailed information on directions, parking, and public transport here.

 

Final Tips

  • Bookmark your favorite sessions in advance
  • Arrive early for popular sessions (rooms fill quickly!)
  • Bring your laptop for Code Jams and hands-on sessions/ workshops
  • Don’t miss the networking moments - some of the best conversations happen in between sessions
  • Follow the agendas closely for last-minute updates

 

See You Soon!

We’re looking forward to an inspiring week of learning, sharing, and connecting.

See you at Code Connect 2026!

entry.title: Connecting Two SAP CAP Projects Without Duplicating Your Data
entry.content:
There's a problem that comes up on almost every multi-project SAP BTP engagement, and I've seen teams solve it in some really messy ways.

You have two CAP projects. One of them — let's call it Project A — owns a set of shared constants, configuration parameters, maybe a job run log. Project B needs to read that data. So what do people do? They copy the table. Or they create an API. Or they hardcode values that really shouldn't be hardcoded.

All of these work. None of them are clean.

The proper solution is HDI cross-container synonyms. Once it's set up, Project B can query Project A's HANA tables directly — same SQL, same CAP service code, no replication, no drift between environments. It looks and feels like a local table. It just happens to live in someone else's HDI container.

I put together a Tech Bite on this recently and figured it's worth writing up properly, because the documentation is scattered and the first time you set it up you will almost certainly hit at least one of the three errors I mention at the end.

The thing nobody explains first: why it's complicated at all

HDI containers are isolated. That's the point of them — each CAP project gets its own schema, its own deploy lifecycle, its own access controls. But that isolation also means Container B can't just say "I want to read from Container A's schema" — because the schema name is dynamically assigned at deployment time. You can't hardcode it.

So SAP's solution is a User-Provided Service, or UPS. It's a simple Cloud Foundry construct — basically a key-value store with no runtime component — that carries the schema name of Project A. You register it with the -t hana tag, declare it in Project B's mta.yaml, and the HDI deployer picks it up during deployment and injects the correct schema wherever you've referenced it.

That's it. That's the whole trick. Everything else — the synonym files, the config files, the grants — is just plumbing on top of that core idea.

What you're actually building in Project B

Once you understand what the UPS is doing, the rest makes sense. You need four types of files:

.hdiconfig tells the deployer which plugins to use. You need two of these — one in db/src/ and one in db/cfg/. It's a small JSON file, easy to get wrong by forgetting one of the two locations.

.hdbsynonym is where you define the local alias. So instead of referencing PROJECT_A_SCHEMA.CONSTANTS_TABLE (which you can't hardcode anyway), you just write CONSTANTS_TABLE and map it to the actual technical object name in Project A. This lives in db/src/.

.hdbsynonymconfig is the file that connects the synonym to the UPS. This is what makes the schema resolution dynamic. It lives in db/cfg/ — deliberately separate from db/src/ to avoid deployment conflicts. The key thing here is that the value you put in schema.configure must start with exactly the same string you used as the key in mta.yaml. More on this when I get to the errors.

.hdbgrants is the access control piece. Project B's container needs to be explicitly granted a role from Project A. That role needs to already exist over in Project A — it's not something you create in Project B. Your Project A team needs to have a role set up that grants SELECT on the tables you need.

The setup, briefly

On the Cloud Foundry side, you create the UPS once:

cf create-user-provided-service ProjectA_UPS \
  -p '{"schema": "YOUR_PROJECT_A_SCHEMA"}' -t hana

Then in Project B's mta.yaml, you declare it as an existing service resource and bind it to the db-deployer module under SERVICE_REPLACEMENTS:

- name: ProjectA-UPS-resource
  group: SERVICE_REPLACEMENTS
  properties:
    key: ProjectA_UPS
    service: ~{service-name}

Your synonym config then references it like this:

"schema.configure": "ProjectA_UPS/schema"

And once everything deploys, your CAP service code doesn't need to know any of this happened. You just query it:

const db = await cds.connect.to("db");
const results = await db.run(
  `SELECT * FROM "CONSTANTS_TABLE" WHERE "PARAM" = ?`, [value]
);

Clean. No different from querying a local table.

The three errors you will probably hit

I'm not going to pretend this always works first time. Here's what goes wrong most often:

"synonym config refers to unknown service replacement" — This one is almost always a key mismatch. The string you put as key in mta.yaml and the prefix before /schema in your .hdbsynonymconfig need to be identical. Character for character. Check both files side by side.

"insufficient privilege" — The role you referenced in .hdbgrants doesn't exist in Project A, or it exists but doesn't include SELECT on the tables you're trying to reach. You'll need to go back to whoever owns Project A and check what roles are actually deployed there. HANA DB Explorer is your friend here.

"object not found" — The object name in your .hdbsynonym is wrong. The technical name of a table in HDI usually follows a namespace pattern with underscores replacing dots — something like HR_HTR_UTILITY_OBJECTS_CONSTANTS_TABLE. The only reliable way to find the exact name is to look it up directly in HANA DB Explorer under Project A's schema.

One thing worth knowing for local development

When you run cds watch locally, you're using SQLite — there's no HDI container, no synonym. So you need a workaround for local testing.

The simplest approach is to create a CDS entity with @CDS.persistence.exists that mirrors the external table's structure, and seed it with a CSV file. It won't be connected to Project A's live data, but it lets you develop and test the rest of your logic without needing a full BTP deployment every time.

Worth the setup

The first time you wire this up it feels like a lot of moving parts. And honestly, it is. But once it's done, it just works — and it works cleanly. No sync jobs, no API calls for what is essentially a config lookup, no risk of the two sides drifting apart over time.

If your project has shared reference data living in another team's HDI container, this is the right way to access it.

Happy to answer questions in the comments if you hit something I haven't covered here.

#SAP #SAPCAP #SAPHANA #BTP #SAPDevelopment #HDI #CloudFoundry #PwC

entry.title: Reading Between the Lines: Under-Documented Fiori Elements Capabilities in SAPUI5 1.149
entry.content:
Every SAPUI5 release ships more than its release notes admit. Between the headline features sits a quieter layer: public, load-bearing capability that is barely documented. You usually meet it while stepping through a -dbg.js at the wrong hour. What follows is a reading of that layer in Fiori Elements 1.149, taken from the framework source. The theme is not a feature list. It is a shift in where behavior belongs, and increasingly that place is the CAP backend.

The center of gravity moved to the backend

The old reflex was to close Fiori Elements gaps in a controller extension. The recent additions are annotation-first: the frontend honors what the service declares.

Event-driven SideEffects (@since 1.148) are the clearest case. A backend event travels over a WebSocket channel and the visible page re-reads exactly the targets the service named. No polling, no refresh code. On the CAP side this is one plugin, @cap-js-community/websocket (github.com/cap-js-community/websocket). Enable both protocols on the service and model a side-effect event:

// srv/service.cds
@ws
@odata
service TravelService {
  entity Travel as projection on my.Travel;

  event travelStatusChanged {
    sideEffectSource : String; // the changed instance path
  }
}

// The plugin derives these automatically from the OData path:
// @Common.WebSocketBaseURL : 'ws/travel'
// @Common.WebSocketChannel #sideEffects : 'sideeffects'

annotate TravelService.Travel with @(
  Common.SideEffects #StatusPush : {
    SourceEvents     : [ 'travelStatusChanged' ],
    TargetProperties : [ 'TravelStatus_code', 'TotalPrice' ]
  }
);

Emit from a handler and every open page re-reads the named targets. cds.queued couples the push to transaction success:

// srv/service.js
module.exports = (srv) => {
  srv.after('UPDATE', 'Travel', async (travel, req) => {
    const svc = cds.queued(srv); // fire only if the tx commits
    await svc.tx(req).emit('travelStatusChanged', {
      sideEffectSource: `/Travel(ID=${travel.ID},IsActiveEntity=true)`
    });
  });
};

Read that carefully: the model, not the app, owns the freshness contract. The frontend hook exists, but its default is to do the sensible thing and get out of the way.

The same instinct runs through AI field recommendations (@since 1.139). The service marks fields with Common.RecommendationRole; the framework renders the placeholders, the value-help grouping, and the accept-all dialog.

annotate TravelService.Travel with {
  CustomerID  @Common.RecommendationRole : #Input;
  Description @Common.RecommendationRole : #Output;
};

What it does not ship is an inference backend. isEnabled returns false and fetchRecommendations is yours:

// ext/Travel.controller.ts (controller extension)
override isEnabled() { return true; }
override async fetchRecommendations(contexts) {
  return contexts.map((ctx) => ({
    context: ctx,
    AIRecommendedFieldPath: 'Description',
    AIRecommendedFieldValue: 'Business trip to Walldorf'
  }));
}

A deliberate seam: the interaction is standardized, the intelligence is delegated. The framework conceded the boring 80% and asks only for the model call.

Collaboration stopped being a bolt-on

Collaborative draft (@since 1.141) lets several users co-author one draft with live field locking over a PCP WebSocket. There is no manifest switch. It activates when the service exposes a draft root, a share action, and a WebSocket base URL. On ABAP RAP that comes for free. On CAP Node.js it did not exist until cap-collaborative-draft (github.com/sleibach/cap-collaborative-draft), a cds-plugin that reproduces the full OData contract. Two annotations on the service entity:

// srv/service.cds
service OrderService {
  @odata.draft.enabled
  @CollaborativeDraft.enabled : true
  entity Orders as projection on my.Orders { *, Items }
}

The plugin injects @Common.DraftRoot.ShareAction, the WebSocket annotations, and the presence and lock contract. It leans on the same @cap-js-community/websocket plugin as a peer dependency. Application code stays out of the protocol and subscribes only where it has domain intent, for example turning an invite into a notification:

const cds = require('@sap/cds');

cds.on('collab-draft:shareInvite', ({ draftUUID, invitedBy, users }) => {
  for (const user of users) sendInviteEmail({ to: user.UserID, draftUUID, invitedBy });
});

On the UI5 side the reuse layer follows the same principle. ExtensionAPI.collaborativeDraft exposes sendPropertyValuesChange, sendLockChange, and their siblings only for the one case that needs them: your code mutating the shared draft and having to tell the other editors. The absence of configuration is the design, not an oversight.

The environment-gated integrations behave the same way. Publishing a page as an Insights card, feeding screen context to Joule, Microsoft Teams contact actions: none ask for a flag. They light up when the host library is present and stay silent otherwise. Stop guarding them with conditionals and let capability detection work.

Building blocks are a public composition API

The sap.fe.macros.* controls graduated from template internals to a supported way to assemble custom pages. In 1.145 an interface hierarchy (ITableColumn, ITableAction, ITableActionOrGroup) formalized what may nest inside a Table, the kind of change that only happens when an API is meant to be extended from outside. MacroAPI implements IFormContent, so a macro drops into a hand-built form:

<macros:Table id="Travels" metaPath="@com.sap.vocabularies.UI.v1.LineItem" contextPath="/Travel">
  <macros:columns>
    <macros:Column key="profit" header="{i18n>profit}" anchor="TotalPrice" placement="After">
      <template><Text text="{= ${Revenue} - ${Cost} }"/></template>
    </macros:Column>
  </macros:columns>
</macros:Table>

And where you once redrew a whole table to change a column, addColumn / removeColumn mutate it at runtime (still experimental; treat the label as a promise about stability, not capability). Rarely mentioned: RichTextEditor and RichTextEditorWithMetadata have been public building blocks since 1.117. If you embedded a third-party editor to bind a long-text property, the platform already had one.

The manifest still hides the most leverage

Much of what teams rebuild by hand is a flag away: multi-view list reports, mass edit with a custom save hook, in-place object-page editing, a per-context paginator rule. All declarative, all in converters/ManifestSettings.ts.

"controlConfiguration": {
  "@com.sap.vocabularies.UI.v1.LineItem": {
    "tableSettings": { "enableMassEdit": true }
  }
}

That file is also a warning: almost no @since tags, and a few switches are labeled temporary in the source (disableCollaborationDraft carries the comment “will be deleted again”). Read the comments as a boundary between what you may lean on and what you may not.

What this adds up to

The pattern is consistent. Model behavior where the data lives, let the framework ship the interaction and ask you only for the domain core, and let features turn on when their environment is ready. Two CAP plugins, @cap-js-community/websocket and cap-collaborative-draft, close the gap that used to make these features ABAP-only.

The practical discipline: before writing the extension, check whether an annotation, a manifest flag, a public building block, or a CAP plugin already does it. In 1.149, more often than you would expect, one does.

Version anchors were verified against the 1.149 framework source. Anything marked experimental or internal can change without notice. The two CAP plugins are community projects under active development; pin exact versions before you depend on them.

entry.title: Media Handling in SAP CAP: Mixing Static and Uploaded Images in Fiori Elements V4
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

Introduction

When building applications with the SAP Cloud Application Programming (CAP) model and SAP Fiori Elements V4, handling media files is a common requirement. Usually, it is straightforward: first define a LargeBinary field, and the framework generates an upload control.

However, things get complicated when we need a single table column to dynamically display images from two different sources:

  1. Static default images (loaded via CSV during deployment).

  2. Custom images uploaded by users via the UI.

This guide covers the complete end-to-end setup to achieve this:

  • Data Modeling — Defining the schema with a "Traffic Cop" virtual field.

  • UI Annotations — Exposing the right fields to the List Report and Object Page.

  • Backend Logic — Intercepting OData V4 requests in Node.js to bypass UI optimizations.

  • HANA Deployment — Resolving common driver version conflicts during deployment.

Why I Wrote This

Recently, I was developing an application on the SAP Business Technology Platform where users needed to browse a catalog of items. Some items had static placeholder images mapped to local CSV paths, while others had high-res photos explicitly uploaded by users.

When I attempted to route these dynamically using a virtual field, my Fiori V4 List Report stubbornly displayed blank "No Image" placeholders. After hours of debugging the OData V4 network payload, I realized Fiori was optimizing my request by stripping out the hidden columns my backend logic relied on. I couldn't find a definitive guide covering this specific Fiori V4 optimization trap, so I'm sharing the bulletproof workaround I built.

 

First, Let's Understand the Fiori V4 Optimization Trap

Before looking at the code, it helps to understand why the standard approach fails in Fiori Elements V4.

Fiori V4 is aggressively optimized for bandwidth. When it loads a List Report, the OData $select query only asks for the columns explicitly visible on the screen. If the backend relies on a virtual field (e.g., displayImageUrl) that calculates its value based on hidden fields (like mediaType or ImageUrl), those hidden fields arrive at the backend handler as undefined. We cannot calculate a dynamic URL if the framework refuses to fetch the underlying data.

To fix this, we don't fight the UI. Instead, we let Fiori request whatever it wants, and we use a custom CAP backend hook to silently query the database for the missing fields before delivering the final payload to the browser.

 

Architecture Overview

This solution relies on a clean separation between the database schema, the UI annotations, and the backend Node.js service.

project-root/
│
├── db/
│   ├── data/
│   │   └── mediahandling.db-Image.csv  ← Initial data linking to static image paths
│   └── schema.cds                      ← Defines the binary, static, and virtual fields
│
├── app/
│   ├── mediahandling/                  ← Your Fiori Elements application
│   │   └── webapp/
│   │       └── images/                 
│   │           ├── image1.jpg          ← The actual static placeholder files
│   │           └── image2.jpg
│   └── annotations.cds                 ← Maps the UI controls for Fiori Elements
│
└── srv/
    ├── catalogService.cds              ← Exposes the OData service
    └── catalogService.js               ← Contains the custom 'READ' interceptor logic

 

Step 1: The Data Model (schema.cds)

We need an entity that stores both the binary file and the static URL, alongside a virtual field to decide which one to render.

Critical Detail: Ensure the @ui.IsImageURL: true annotation is applied directly to the virtual field here in the schema. This instructs Fiori V4 to natively allocate an avatar control in the List Report.

namespace mediahandling.db;
using { cuid, managed } from '@sap/cds/common';

entity Image: cuid, managed {
    Name        : String(100);
    Description : String(255);
    
    // Holds the static CSV path (e.g., 'images/default.jpg')
    ImageUrl    : String; 

    // The actual uploaded file data
    @Core.MediaType  : mediaType
    image       : LargeBinary;

    // The file extension/type (e.g., 'image/jpeg')
    @Core.IsMediaType: true
    mediaType   : String;

    // The "Traffic Cop" virtual field for Fiori UI display
    @ui.IsImageURL: true
    virtual displayImageUrl : String;
}

 

Step 2: Initializing the Mock Data (CSV)

To test this, we need to load initial records into the database. Create a file named mediahandling.db-Image.csv inside the db/data/ folder.

ID;Name;Description;ImageUrl
22222222-2222-2222-2222-222222222201;Sunset Boulevard;A beautiful vibrant sunset over the city boulevard.;images/image1.jpg
22222222-2222-2222-2222-222222222202;Mountain Peak;The highest snow-capped peak in the northern mountain range.;images/image2.jpg

 

Step 3: UI Annotations (annotations.cds)

In the UI annotations, we expose the virtual displayImageUrl field in the UI.LineItem (List Report) so the dynamic image renders in the table. We expose the actual image (LargeBinary) field inside the UI.FieldGroup (Object Page) so the user gets a file upload prompt when creating or editing a record.

using MyService as service from '../../srv/catalogService';

annotate service.Image with @(
    UI.FieldGroup #GeneratedGroup : {
        $Type : 'UI.FieldGroupType',
        Data : [
            { $Type : 'UI.DataField', Label : 'Name', Value : Name },
            { $Type : 'UI.DataField', Label : 'Description', Value : Description },
            { $Type : 'UI.DataField', Label : 'Upload Image', Value : image }
        ],
    },
    UI.Facets : [
        {
            $Type : 'UI.ReferenceFacet',
            ID : 'GeneratedFacet1',
            Label : 'General Information',
            Target : '@UI.FieldGroup#GeneratedGroup',
        },
    ],
    UI.LineItem : [
        { $Type : 'UI.DataField', Label : 'Name', Value : Name },
        { $Type : 'UI.DataField', Label : 'Image', Value : displayImageUrl }, 
        { $Type : 'UI.DataField', Label : 'Description', Value : Description }
    ],
    UI.HeaderInfo:{
        $Type : 'UI.HeaderInfoType',
        TypeName : 'Image Record',
        TypeNamePlural : 'Image Records',
        Title : { $Type: 'UI.DataField', Value: ID },
        Description : { $Type: 'UI.DataField', Value: Name },
        ImageUrl : displayImageUrl 
    }
);

 

Step 4: The Backend Interceptor (catalogService.js)

This is where the magic happens. We intercept the after('READ') event. If we detect that Fiori stripped our necessary columns (mediaType and ImageUrl), we immediately execute a lightweight database query to fetch them for the visible rows. Finally, we route the URL accordingly.

const cds = require('@sap/cds');

module.exports = cds.service.impl(async function() {
    // Dynamically grab the actual service base path (e.g., /odata/v4/my)
    const srvPath = this.path;

    this.after('READ', 'Image', async (data, req) => {
        const images = Array.isArray(data) ? data : [data];
        if (images.length === 0) return;

        // If Fiori stripped the columns to save bandwidth, we step in.
        if (images[0].mediaType === undefined && images[0].ImageUrl === undefined) {
            
            // Grab the IDs of the rows currently visible on the screen
            const ids = images.map(img => img.ID);
            
            // Query the database directly to fetch the missing fields
            const dbData = await cds.tx(req).run(
                SELECT.from(req.target)
                      .columns('ID', 'mediaType', 'ImageUrl', 'IsActiveEntity')
                      .where({ ID: { 'in': ids } })
            );

            // Stitch the missing data back into the original payload
            images.forEach(img => {
                const match = dbData.find(d => d.ID === img.ID);
                if (match) {
                    img.mediaType = match.mediaType;
                    img.ImageUrl = match.ImageUrl;
                    img.IsActiveEntity = match.IsActiveEntity;
                }
            });
        }

        // Execute the Traffic Cop logic (guaranteed to have the data)
        images.forEach(image => {
            if (image.mediaType) {
                // Route to the OData media stream for uploaded binaries
                const isActive = image.IsActiveEntity !== false;
                image.displayImageUrl = `${srvPath}/Image(ID=${image.ID},IsActiveEntity=${isActive})/image`;
            } else if (image.ImageUrl) {
                // Route to the static CSV path
                image.displayImageUrl = image.ImageUrl;
            } else {
                // Fallback placeholder
                image.displayImageUrl = 'https://dummyimage.com/200x200/cccccc/000000.png&text=No+Image';
            }
        });
    });
});

 

Output:

 

Note: Deploying to SAP HANA Cloud

When transitioning from a local SQLite setup to SAP HANA Cloud, we may encounter an NPM dependency error when starting the hybrid profile.

If the project is running @SAP/cds version 9, but the latest @cap-js/hana driver expects version 10, the standard npm install will fail with an ERESOLVE conflict. We can safely bypass this strict peer dependency check and link the driver by running the following:

npm install @cap-js/hana --legacy-peer-deps
Once installed, cds watch --profile hybrid command will connect to the HANA instance seamlessly.
 

Conclusion

By shifting the data-retrieval logic into the Node.js backend rather than relying on messy UI workarounds or forced hidden columns, we maintain the strict performance benefits of Fiori Elements V4 while delivering a seamless, dynamic user experience.

entry.title: SAP Developer News, July 9th, 2026
entry.content:
ITEMS

Live from WeAreDevelopers

- WeAreDevelopers World Congress https://www.wearedevelopers.com/world-congress

CAP June 2026 release (cds 10)

- Release notes https://cap.cloud.sap/docs/releases/2026/jun26

ABAP Cleaner in VS Code!

- https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=SAPOSS.abap-cleaner

Live streams at Code Connect next week

- Code Connect https://code-connect.dev/
- UI5con https://openui5.org/ui5con/
- UI5con agenda https://openui5.org/ui5con/program.html
- UI5con 2026 Live from the Main Stage https://www.youtube.com/live/CMPudw4scSE
- reCAP https://recap-conf.dev/
- reCAP agenda https://recap-conf.dev/program.html
- reCAP Live from the Main Stage https://broadcast.sap.com/go/reCAP
- reCAP 2026 - Streaming Sidetrack https://teams.microsoft.com/meet/347149883889697?p=ZkR4erzGjpPRtZ6zJh

Additional CAP highlights and MCP Gateway

- Building MCP Servers with SAP Integration Suite: What to Design, Not Just How to Connect https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/building-mcp-servers-with-sap-integration-suite-what-to-design-not-just-how/ba-p/14435261
- Design APIs and MCP servers https://help.sap.com/docs/integration-suite/isuite-integrations-and-apis/design-apis?locale=en-US
- Release calendar for SAP Integration Suite https://me.sap.com/notes/3418154

Did You Know? #25 cds add test

- The cds add command https://cap.cloud.sap/docs/tools/cds-cli#cds-add

CHAPTER TITLES

00:00 Intro
00:07 Live from WeAreDevelopers
02:16 CAP June 2026 release (cds 10)
13:14 ABAP Cleaner in VS Code!
14:37 Live streams at Code Connect next week
15:05 Additional CAP highlights and MCP Gateway
17:20 DYK #25 cds add test

TRANSCRIPT

[Intro]
This is the Developer News for July 9th 2026.

[Tom, Sheena, Shilpa, Witalij]
Hi, we're coming to you this week from WeAreDevelopers conference in Berlin, which is a little bit of a difference for us developer advocates because we're actually mostly attending a conference instead of delivering one. So it's our opportunity to connect with other developers, particularly non-SAP developers from all around the world. I'm giving a session on Friday with Michael Ameling, where I'll be doing a preview of the new developers.sap.com that's all AI-powered - we'll show you more of in the coming weeks. What are the rest of you looking forward to?

Yeah we just now attended one session on Microsoft Fabric, it was really exciting, and we also got to know the latest advanced technologies that we can look forward to. So it was really exciting to be part of that session. The line was a little too long to get into the session.

That is the challenging part, to get into the sessions, but for me the interesting part is AI and agents because, it is not a surprise, but to be outside of the SAP ecosystem is what I'm interested in too, and get to learn more of what's happening outside of SAP.

So have you tried to find any sessions without AI or agents in the name?

No, all agents. I think that was the qualifier, probably. OK, and for me it's actually not the first time I'm attending this conference. I was here in 2019 and it is really good to be back here. This time SAP has a big presence here, we are actually one of the sponsors, we have our place on the show floor, we have our sessions as Thomas mentioned, he's going to do one with Michael Ameling but as well we have some other SAP colleagues delivering sessions based on the experience from their work, working with UI for example, and with user experience in this exciting AI era. So all in all, "no coffee, no code" but unfortunately the line for coffee is the longest one here, so if you don't see me coding this week, that's just because I cannot stand in this line and get my coffee.

[DJ]
cds 10 is out! That is, the next major release of the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model, also known as cds 10, was published last week.

This particular major release marks many milestones and is packed full of goodness.

Within the limitations of these news items, I can only give a brief overview, and you should definitely head over to the release notes which are very readable and full of details.

Some of the features and highlights are marked as Alpha or Beta ... so please pay attention to these status badges when working through the content.

Now, like other releases, the notes for this release are organised into different categories. There's the main major release upgrade category of course, a set of important highlights, notes on changes and improvements for both the Node.js and Java runtimes, and some updates for multitenancy services as well.

So, let's start with the very essence of this major release - the upgrade and migration context, and what tht implies.

Each of the packages that make up the entire framework have had major release version upgrades.
As the release notes say, don't mix old and new major versions. Instead, embrace change and try out the new cds upgrade tool which can help you.

The runtime support is also changing, with both Node.js and Java.

And before leaving this upgrade context, I must highlight the facilities that the CAP team have put together for this essential part of the software lifecycle - upgrades and migration. There's a new Migration Guide to help you get to cds 10 ... and a bleeding edge new 'cds upgrade' facility, in Alpha, to help with upgrades.

So next, let's have a look at some of the highlights, of which there are many in this release. Some of them are either in Alpha or Beta status, but they're there!

Let me enumerate them briefly. There's now support for aggregate data in hierarchies, useful for product catalogs, org charts, bills of materials and similar scenarios. It's available right now with the Java runtime, and support for Node.js is coming soon.

For VS Code users there's a new Project Explorer to help you visually navigate through your project and model's structures.

Now, while this has been hinted at before this advent of cds 10, there's a new protocol which I'm going to describe as "CAP native", and that's now available, and it's called HCQL - CQL conveyed over HTTP. Well suited for CAP-to-CAP integrations, and available just by adding an @hcql annotation, in the true CAP tradition of magic-made-simple.

There are early AI related goodies too, in the form of CAP-level (Calesi-style) agents, and also a CAP skills library, because, as we know, focusing on intent and best practice in development is essential.

There's a new Data Inspector, which comes in the form of a plugin and allows the controlled inspection of data in the entities exposed by your services.

Now, event queues in CAP have also been given lots of love, in the form of a new scheduling API, improved processing, and an overhaul of the documentation.

Now, last December we saw the first public Beta of the 'cds export' command which we can use to create API client packages from CDS service definitions. With this cds 10 release, that command is now generally available.

Now there are some more highlights in this cds 10 release, but my good friend and colleague Antonio is going to cover those in another news item in this news episode, so stay tuned!

Let me now turn to the Node.js runtime context where there are also plenty of items for us to celebrate.

First up, consolidated service APIs. What does that mean? Well, the generic CRUD (Create Read Update Delete) handler now returns the same shape of data for all write operations. That means app, db and HCQL-proxied remote services all look and behave the same way, which is great for uniform and minimised handler code variations.

Now, AI agents are great, but one thing they don't know much about is Fiori draft data. So working with active data has been simplified and consolidated - making it easier for everyone, us humans and our AI overlords, to work in a draft-first context.

A massive piece of work that the team has undertaken over the last months, culminating in what we have with cds 10, is a significant move towards a greater embrace of modern tools and native libraries. Very briefly - the testing framework Vitest is now used in favour of Jest, which will be abandoned longer term. ECMAScript Modules (that's ESM to you and me) is the official standardised system for packages and reuse, and CAP is now embracing that as the default module system. Third party dependencies are being dropped in favour of native or built-in alternatives. For example this calendar year already has seen a move to a Node-native SQLite driver (which replaces better-sqlite3) and to the Node-native Fetch API (which replaces Axios, and in development scenarios, also removes the need for the SAP Cloud SDK).

This work to embrace modern techniques, native libraries and the newest stable standards is fantastic. An important "side effect" is also something we can celebrate - a significantly reduced set of dependencies (and thus a smaller install footprint and code surface area).

Now last but certainly not least in the Node.js category is some news on the Telemetry plugin, which is now at version 2 after a major update which also brings support for OpenTelemetry SDK 2.0.

Next, let's look at the Java runtime category, which has now reached major version 5. Lots of new features, including, importantly, support for Spring Boot 4.

There are some new minimum versions of major dependencies that you need to take care of.

With CAP Java 5 there are some changes that are not backwards compatible (as in any major release), but the migration is made easier for us with a specific migration guide and OpenRewrite recipes for automated code adjustments.

Now, there are changes to CDS property defaults to align them with recommended settings for production use.

The API methods that were deprecated in the previous major release and marked as 'forRemoval' ... have now been removed.

CAP Java 5 now defaults to JDK 25, which is a long-term-support version. The SapMachine 25 JDK with the SAP Java buildpack is recommended.

With this new major CAP Java release, search is harmonised with both CAP Node.js and RAP, and by default uses the SAP HANA search syntax (for which there is now full support). Fuzzy search is also now enabled by default on SAP HANA, with improved support for UUIDs and large strings, and available in hierarchy queries.

Now, there's also much faster typed data access available. Instead of using a dynamic proxy to handle the underlying data map for accessor interfaces, it's now possible to have implementation classes generated. This resulting in up to 10 times the performance.

Previously, CAP Java used Apache's Olingo library for OData support. Following Olingo's retirement last year, there's now built-in support for OData, that's no mean feat! That of course also means fewer dependencies and it also brings improved performance.

There's also support for creating active versions of draft-enabled entities, similar to what we learned about earlier in the Node.js runtime context.

There are also plenty more features that come with CAP Java 5. Check them out in the release notes.

But to round out the release notes overview for this cds 10 version, there are a couple of points of note in the context of MTX services. First, there's now a stricter annotation validation for extensions. And second, migration support to go from @SAP/cds-mtx to @SAP/cds-mtxs has been removed.

That pretty much wraps up things for this June 2026 major release, congratulations to the entire CAP team for a huge set of new features and improvements. And don't forget to check out Antonio's item in this news episode for those other highlights.

[Rich]
Hey friends, Rich Heilman here. I hope everyone is staying cool, staying out of the heat. Good news for ABAP developers this week. ABAP Cleaner is now available as a VS Code extension, bringing its powerful code formatting capabilities out of Eclipse and into the modern editor of choice for many developers. So, what does it do? ABAP Cleaner applies over 100 rules to your code with a single keyboard shortcut. Automatically cleaning up formatting, modernising syntax, and even flagging unused variables. The result is clean and consistent code across your entire team without anyone having to argue about style. You can run it as a quick one-shot format, or drop into interactive mode to choose exactly which rules apply to your codebase. If your team already has a configuration setup in Eclipse, you can import those rule variants directly. No need to start from scratch. The setup does a Java runtime and a bit of environment variable configuration, but once it's running, you get the same functionality that ABAP developers have relied on in ADT in Eclipse - now in a much lighter, faster editor. If you're doing ABAP development in VS Code, this one's worth installing. Check it out today.

[Ajay]
Hello everyone. Code Connect is next week, from 13th to 16th July 2026. For all of you who cannot join us in person, there are live streams available for UI5con and reCAP, from the main stage. Please check out the agenda and streaming links available for UI5con and reCAP and join us online during the Code Connect event. Links to the live streams and agendas will be available in the description.

[Antonio]
¡Hola, SAP Developers. As you already know, the June 2026 CAP release is huge. DJ has already shared many of the highlights and I just want to mention a couple more. There is a new AI plugin, in Alpha, which simplifies integration with SAP AI Core. It will automatically resolve service bindings, manage resource groups, and AI model deployments. And it provides a standard AI Core service to interact with AI Core programatically. The new Data Privacy plugin, in Beta, for Node.js, which provides out of the box integration with the SAP Data Privacy Integration Service. Based on the personal data and ILM annotations in the data model the plugin will automatically expose two endpoints that can be consumed by the service. Now, related to the integration space, CAP Node.js now supports the Event Mesh capability in SAP Integration Suite, as an alternative to the standalone SAP Event Mesh, meaning that you can now easily use it as an event broken in your CAP applications. There is the new MCP Protocol Adapter, which allows exposing existing CAP services to AI agents and LLM powered as just another protocol. Just install the plugin and annotate services with @McP, et voila, your CAP service exposes MCP functionality. Extremely powerful and easy to implement. I already used it in a CAP service that I'm developing while I was getting familiar with the new feature of SAP Integration Suite. Yes, what we've all been waiting for - the new MCP Gateway is now available as part of Integration Suite. It is part of the new Integration Cell Runtime which is a cloud native runtime purposely built for designing, deploying and operating APIs and MCP servers. It is GA now and being rolled out in the Enhanced and Premium editions of SAP Integration Suite. Not just that, but it is also available in the BTP Trial environment. What are yoiu waiting for? Go check it out. Hasta luego!

[Ajay]
Did you know that cds add does generate the best practices of the test implementation of your CAP service? One can rapidly create a sample CAP project with cds init bookshop --add sample. Run cds add test in the terminal to add test suite files for all the defined CAP services in your existing project. It creates the latest boilerplate files and structures required for you to get started with your test implementation in a jiffy. For each services it generates a sample test for the GET request for an entity and a test for any action defined in the service. This also adds the test dependencies to your project. It uses the Node test runner for the tests. Install your dependencies and just run npm t to execute your tests. The generated test implementation does need to be tweaked for the valid test data. CAP also provides a command npx cds test to mute the verbose server output and lets you focus only on the failing tests. Use -u to unmute the server output when you do not need them. Enjoy your CAP tests!

entry.title: 🐍 July Developer Challenge Week 2 — Roll & Move
entry.content:
Week 2 — Roll & Move

Challenge series: Snake & Ladder Developer Challenge | Week 2 of 4 Dates: 13–19 July 2026

The Setup

You have a board. You have a player state machine. This week we will make it move.

This week you implement the bound action handlers that power the game: dice rolling, position calculation, and snake/ladder logic. CAP's @flow.status machinery will automatically enforce that moves only happen in the right state — your job is to write the business logic inside each handler.

By the end of this week you can play a full single-player game from the command line using plain OData calls.

What You'll Build This Week

  • Handler implementations for rollDice, confirmMove, blockPlayer, unblockPlayer, and winGame
  • Snake and ladder position resolution (including double-headed snake logic)
  • HTTP 409 proof: demonstrating that calling an action in the wrong state is rejected by CAP
  • An end-to-end single-player run reaching square 100

How CAP Enforces State Transitions

Before your handler code runs, CAP checks the current TurnStatus of the player entity against the @from annotation on the action. If the status doesn't match, CAP returns:

HTTP/1.1 409 Conflict
{
  "error": {
    "code": "409",
    "message": "Action 'rollDice' is not allowed in current status 'Waiting'"
  }
}

No code from you. No if (status !== 'Playing') throw .... The annotation does it.

After your handler returns (without throwing), CAP updates the status field to the @TO value. Again — no code from you.

This is the key insight of the week: your handler only contains business logic, not state management.

Step-by-Step

1. Create the service implementation file

Create srv/game-service.js:

const cds = require('@sap/cds')

module.exports = class GameService extends cds.ApplicationService {
  async init() {

    const { Players, BoardSquares } = this.entities

    // ── startTurn ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // @from: [#Waiting]  →  CAP validates status before this runs
    // @to: #Playing      →  CAP updates status after this returns
    // No business logic needed — CAP's @flow.status handles the transition.
    this.on('startTurn', 'Players', async (req) => {
      // Intentionally empty: the @from/@to annotations do all the work.
    })

    // ── rollDice ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // @from: [#Playing]  →  CAP validates status before this runs
    // @to: #Moving       →  CAP updates status after this returns
    this.on('rollDice', 'Players', async (req) => {
      const player = await SELECT.one.from(Players).where({ ID: req.params[0].ID })
      if (!player) return req.error(404, 'Player not found')

      const roll = Math.ceil(Math.random() * 6)
      let newPosition = player.position + roll

      // Can't overshoot square 100 — must land exactly
      if (newPosition > 100) {
        await UPDATE(Players).set({ lastRoll: roll }).where({ ID: player.ID })
        const needed = 100 - player.position
        return req.reject(409, `Need exactly ${needed} to finish. Rolled ${roll}. No move.`)
      }

      // Resolve snake or ladder on the new square
      const square = await SELECT.one.from(BoardSquares).where({ square: newPosition })
      let finalPosition = newPosition
      let event = null

      if (square?.ladderTo) {
        finalPosition = square.ladderTo
        event = { type: 'ladder', 'from': newPosition, 'to': finalPosition }
      } else if (square?.snakeTo) {
        finalPosition = square.snakeTo
        event = square.isDoubleHead
          ? { type: 'doubleSnake', 'from': newPosition, 'to': finalPosition }
          : { type: 'snake', 'from': newPosition, 'to': finalPosition }
      }

      // Persist position and last roll
      await UPDATE(Players)
        .set({ position: finalPosition, lastRoll: roll })
        .where({ ID: player.ID })

      // Emit domain event so subscribers (Week 3) can react
      if (event) await this.emit('BoardEvent', { playerID: player.ID, ...event })

      // Use req.reply() — not return — to pin the response value before CAP's
      // @flow.status after-handler runs. A plain `return` gets cleared by the
      // flow machinery, producing HTTP 204 instead of the expected 200 + body.
      req.reply({ roll, position: finalPosition, event: event?.type ?? 'normal' })
    })

    // ── confirmMove ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // @from: [#Moving]  →  normal end of a turn, hands off to next player (Week 3)
    // @to: #Waiting     →  player waits for their next turn
    this.on('confirmMove', 'Players', async (req) => {
      // Week 3 will add turn rotation here.
      // For now: just let CAP apply @to: #Waiting
    })

    // ── blockPlayer ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // @from: [#Moving]  →  called when player lands on a double-headed snake
    // @to: #Blocked     →  player skips their next turn
    this.on('blockPlayer', 'Players', async (req) => {
      // Business logic: the position was already updated in rollDice.
      // This action just transitions state — CAP handles the @to: #Blocked update.
      req.info('Player is blocked and will skip their next turn.')
    })

    // ── unblockPlayer ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // @from: [#Blocked]   →  called at the start of a blocked player's next turn
    // @to: $flow.previous →  CAP restores the status to whatever it was before #Blocked
    this.on('unblockPlayer', 'Players', async (req) => {
      // CAP's $flow.previous does the work — no code needed here.
      // The player's status returns to #Waiting automatically.
    })

    // ── winGame ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // @from: [#Moving]  →  called when player.position === 100
    // @to: #Finished    →  player has won
    this.on('winGame', 'Players', async (req) => {
      const player = await SELECT.one.from(Players).where({ ID: req.params[0].ID })
      if (!player) return req.error(404, 'Player not found')

      // Mark the session as finished with this player as winner
      await UPDATE('snakeladder.GameSessions')
        .set({
          finishedAt: new Date().toISOString(),
          winner_ID: player.ID
        })
        .where({ ID: player.session_ID })

      await this.emit('GameWon', { playerID: player.ID, sessionID: player.session_ID })
    })

    return super.init()
  }
}

If you notice rollDice action response looks different from how it was defined last week.

Lets adapt it as shown in srv/game-service.cds

  @from: [ #Playing ]
  @to: #Moving
  action rollDice() returns { roll: Integer; position: Integer; event: String; };

 

2. Add the domain events to your service CDS

Add these to the bottom of srv/game-service.cds:

// Domain events — emitted by handlers, consumed in Week 3
event BoardEvent {
  playerID : UUID;
  type     : String;   // 'ladder' | 'snake' | 'doubleSnake'
  from  : Integer;  // 'from' is a CDS reserved word — escape with ![]
  ![to]    : Integer;
}

event GameWon {
  playerID  : UUID;
  sessionID : UUID;
}

An error is intentionally introduced in the snippet above with the hint.  

3. Test the state machine manually

Start the server:

cds watch

First, create a player using the REST API or the Fiori Launchpad at http://localhost:4004. Then test the status flow:

Note on service path: CAP derives the OData path from the service name. GameService maps to /odata/v4/game (lowercase, stripped of the "Service" suffix). All example requests below use this path. You can confirm the mounted path in the cds watch console output: serving GameService { at: '/odata/v4/game' }.

Step 1 — Start a turn (transitions Waiting → Playing):

POST http://localhost:4004/odata/v4/game/Players(ID=<your-player-id>)/startTurn
Content-Type: application/json
{}

Expected response: HTTP 204 No Content (empty body). This is correct — startTurn has no return type, so CAP applies the @TO: #Playing transition silently. Verify the state changed with a GET:

GET http://localhost:4004/odata/v4/game/Players(ID=<your-player-id>)?$select=TurnStatus

You should see "TurnStatus": "Playing".

Step 2 — Roll the dice (transitions Playing → Moving):

POST http://localhost:4004/odata/v4/game/Players(ID=<your-player-id>)/rollDice
Content-Type: application/json
{}

Expected response: HTTP 200 with the dice roll, new position, and board event:

{
  "@context": "$metadata#GameService.rollDiceType",
  "roll": 4,
  "position": 38,
  "event": "ladder"
}

The event field is "normal", "ladder", "snake", or "doubleSnake". The position is the final square after any snake or ladder has been applied — so you can read the outcome directly from the action response without a follow-up GET.

Why req.reply() not return? CAP's @flow.status machinery runs an after-handler that updates the status field. If you use a plain return, that after-handler clears the response value and the client gets HTTP 204 No Content instead. Calling req.reply({...}) pins the response before the flow handler runs, so the body reaches the client.

Step 3 — Prove the 409 rejection:

Without calling confirmMove first, try to call rollDice again. CAP will return:

HTTP/1.1 409 Conflict
{
  "error": {
    "code": "409",
    "message": "Action 'rollDice' is not allowed in current status 'Moving'"
  }
}

This is the framework working exactly as designed. Screenshot this — it's your Week 2 submission evidence.

Step 4 — Confirm the move (transitions Moving → Waiting):

POST http://localhost:4004/odata/v4/game/Players(ID=<your-player-id>)/confirmMove
Content-Type: application/json
{}

Expected response: HTTP 204 No Content (empty body). confirmMove has no return type — the empty response is correct. Verify with a GET that TurnStatus is now "Waiting".

4. Test the double-headed snake

Manually set a player to position 88 (one roll away from square 89, a double-headed snake head):

PATCH http://localhost:4004/odata/v4/GameService/Players(ID=<your-player-id>)
Content-Type: application/json
{ "position": 88 }

Note: position is @readonly in the service — for testing, update it directly via the db entity or temporarily remove @readonly.

Then start a turn and roll — if you roll 1, you land on 89 (a double-headed snake head). Your position should move to 50 (the shared tail). The BoardEvent event should fire with type: 'doubleSnake'.

Follow up by calling blockPlayer to transition to #Blocked, then unblockPlayer to see $flow.previous restore the state.

The $flow.previous Moment

When you call unblockPlayer and see the status return to its previous value automatically — that's CAP's state history at work. No code, no stored "previousStatus" field. The framework tracks it.

Note: $flow.previous restores whatever status the entity held before the transition to #Blocked. In this flow, blockPlayer is called from #Moving, so unblockPlayer restores #Moving — not #Waiting. The game loop then calls confirmMove to finish the turn. If you call blockPlayer from a different status in future extensions, $flow.previous will restore that one instead.

Stretch Goal

Add a skipTurn action that can be called when a player is #Blocked, as an alternative to unblockPlayer, that uses @TO: $flow.previous as well but also increments a turnsBlocked counter on the player:

@from: [ #Blocked ]
@to: $flow.previous
action skipTurn();
this.on('skipTurn', 'Players', async (req) => {
  const player = await SELECT.one.from(Players).where({ ID: req.params[0].ID })
  await UPDATE(Players)
    .set({ turnsBlocked: (player.turnsBlocked || 0) + 1 })
    .where({ ID: player.ID })
})

How to Submit

Reply to the Week 2 blog post on SAP Community with a comment containing:

  1. Your srv/game-service.js handler implementations (paste as a JS code block)
  2. A screenshot showing the HTTP 409 response when calling rollDice in the wrong state
  3. A screenshot or OData response showing your player's position after landing on a snake or ladder
  4. One sentence: what happens if you call winGame on a player whose position is not 100? How does your implementation handle it?

What's Next

In Week 3 — Multiplayer & Events, you add a second player, implement turn rotation, and wire up a CAP event subscriber that automatically advances the game state when a move is confirmed.

entry.title: Fixing Sort, Group & Reset in Fiori Elements with CAP CDS What's Not Standard and What's Needed
entry.content:
The Problem

In a SAP CAP Fiori Elements List Report or Object Page application, you might find that Sort, Group, and Reset personalization features do not work out of the box, especially when your entity uses navigation properties (associations) as display columns.

Symptoms

  • Clicking Sort on a column throws a backend error or does nothing
  • Group dialog shows columns that cannot be grouped, causing OData errors
  • Reset or Restore button does not work, or personalization state is lost on page refresh
  • Search works but sort and group personalization is broken

Example Scenario

Consider an entity that displays details from an associated entity:

entity OrderItems {
  key order   : Association to Orders;
  key product : Association to Products;
}

Your @ui.LineItem annotation displays navigation properties:

annotate MyService.OrderItems with @(UI: {
  LineItem: [
    { Value: product_ID },          // flat key - works fine
    { Value: product.name },        // navigation property - breaks sort
    { Value: product.category },    // navigation property - breaks sort
    { Value: order.customerName }   // navigation property - breaks sort
  ]
});

Fiori Elements will show all these columns in the Sort and Group personalization dialog. But the OData backend cannot sort on navigation properties like product.name. Attempting to do so results in a runtime error.

What is Standard vs. What You Need to Add

Standard (works out of the box)

Feature Works For

SortingFlat or direct properties of the entity (e.g., product_ID, order_ID)
GroupingFlat or direct properties
Table rendering@ui.LineItem, @ui.PresentationVariant annotations
Search@CDS.search annotation on the entity

NOT Standard - You Must Configure These

The following three fixes are required when your table displays navigation properties.

Fix 1: SortRestrictions for Navigation Properties

When your @ui.LineItem displays columns from associations, Fiori Elements will offer these in the Sort and Group dialog by default. But the OData backend cannot sort on navigation properties.

The Fix

Add Capabilities.SortRestrictions.NonSortableProperties to exclude navigation properties from sort and group:

annotate MyService.OrderItems with @(
  Capabilities: {
    SortRestrictions: {
      NonSortableProperties: [
        product_ID,
        product.name,
        product.category,
        product.description,
        order.customerName,
        order.status
      ]
    }
  }
);

What This Does

  • Tells Fiori Elements not to offer these columns in the Sort or Group personalization dialog
  • Prevents runtime OData errors when users try to sort
  • The columns still display in the table. They just cannot be sorted or grouped.

When to Use

  • Any time your @ui.LineItem references a navigation property (e.g., product.name, order.type.description)
  • Deep navigation paths like order.statusRef.name

Fix 2: data-sap-ui-flexibility-services for Personalization Persistence

By default, Fiori Elements personalization (sort, group, filter variants) requires a flexibility service to store the user's settings. Without it, the Reset button may not work and personalization state is lost on refresh.

The Fix

Add the following attributes to your app's index.html bootstrap script:

<script
    id="sap-ui-bootstrap"
    src="https://sapui5.hana.ondemand.com/resources/sap-ui-core.js"
    crossorigin="anonymous"
    data-sap-ui-theme="sap_horizon"
    data-sap-ui-resourceroots='{
        "com.mycompany.myapp": "./"
    }'
    data-sap-ui-oninit="module:sap/ui/core/ComponentSupport"
    data-sap-ui-compatVersion="edge"
    data-sap-ui-async="true"
    data-sap-ui-frameOptions="trusted"
    data-sap-ui-flexibility-services='[{"connector": "SessionStorageConnector"}]'
></script>

Key Attributes

Attribute Purpose

crossorigin="anonymous"Required for proper CORS handling of the UI5 library resources
data-sap-ui-flexibility-servicesConfigures where personalization state is stored
SessionStorageConnectorStores personalization in browser session storage (development or standalone)

Production vs. Development

  • Development or Standalone: Use SessionStorageConnector. It stores personalization in browser session storage.
  • Production on BTP Launchpad: The SAPUI5 Flexibility Service handles this automatically via the Launchpad shell. You typically do not need this attribute when deployed to a managed Launchpad.
  • Standalone production apps: Use SessionStorageConnector or configure a persistence backend.

Fix 3: @ui.HiddenFilter for Non-Filterable Fields

If your entity exposes fields via associations that should not appear in the filter bar's "Adapt Filters" dialog:

annotate MyService.Products with {
  description  @UI.HiddenFilter;
  category     @UI.HiddenFilter;
  manufacturer @UI.HiddenFilter;
  weight       @UI.HiddenFilter;
}

What This Does

  • Hides these fields from the "Adapt Filters" dialog in the List Report
  • Prevents users from trying to filter on fields that may not be efficiently queryable
  • The fields still display in tables. They are just not offered as filter options.

Complete Example

Here is a complete annotation setup for an entity with navigation properties:

// Entity definition with associations
entity OrderItems {
  key order   : Association to Orders;
  key product : Association to Products;
      quantity : Integer;
}

// Annotations
annotate MyService.OrderItems with @(
  // Prevent sort and group on navigation properties
  Capabilities: {
    SortRestrictions: {
      NonSortableProperties: [
        product_ID,
        product.name,
        product.category,
        product.description,
        order.customerName
      ]
    }
  },
  UI: {
    PresentationVariant: { Visualizations: ['@UI.LineItem'] },
    LineItem: [
      { Value: product_ID,          ![@UI.Importance]: #High },
      { Value: product.name,        ![@UI.Importance]: #High },
      { Value: product.category,    ![@UI.Importance]: #Medium },
      { Value: order.customerName,  ![@UI.Importance]: #Medium },
      { Value: quantity }
    ]
  }
);

// Hide non-filterable fields from the Adapt Filters dialog
annotate MyService.Products with {
  description  @UI.HiddenFilter;
  category     @UI.HiddenFilter;
  manufacturer @UI.HiddenFilter;
}

Summary

Feature Standard? What to Add Where

Sort on flat propertiesYesNothingN/A
Sort on navigation propertiesNo, failsSortRestrictions.NonSortablePropertiesCDS annotations
Group on flat propertiesYesNothingN/A
Group on navigation propertiesNo, failsSortRestrictions.NonSortablePropertiesCDS annotations
Reset personalizationMay not workdata-sap-ui-flexibility-servicesindex.html
Personalization persistenceLost on refreshSessionStorageConnectorindex.html
Hide fields from filter dialogShows all@ui.HiddenFilterCDS annotations
CORS for UI5 resourcesMay warncrossorigin="anonymous"index.html

Key Takeaway

Fiori Elements is powerful but assumes your OData entity is flat. When you use CAP associations and display navigation properties in tables, you must explicitly tell the framework which columns are non-sortable and non-groupable. Without this, the personalization dialogs offer options that crash at runtime.

The three things to always check:

  1. SortRestrictions for every navigation property in your @ui.LineItem
  2. flexibility-services in your index.html for personalization to work
  3. @ui.HiddenFilter for fields that should not be in the filter dialog

References

  • CAP CDS Annotations: Capabilities
  • Fiori Elements: Configuring Tables
  • SAPUI5 Flexibility Services

entry.title: How To Create Value Help In CAPM.
entry.content:
Hi Everyone,

In this blog, I'll walk you through one of the easiest ways to implement Value Help (F4 Help) in a SAP CAPM application.

While developing SAP Fiori applications, we often have fields such as Category, Department, Country, Employee, Supplier, and many others where users should select values instead of typing them manually. If users enter values manually, there is always a chance of entering incorrect or inconsistent data.

To solve this problem, SAP provides Value Help (F4 Help), which allows users to choose values from a predefined list. This not only improves the user experience but also ensures that only valid data is saved.

In this blog, I'll demonstrate how to create a simple Value Help for the Category field by using a lookup entity, exposing it through a CAP service, and configuring the required CDS annotations.

To Demonstrate this I have Created a simple CAPM application, folder structure of this Looks like this below image



Step 1 – Create Database Entities

First, open the db folder and create a schema.cds file.

In this example, I created two entities:

  • Products
  • Categories

The Products entity stores the product details, while the Categories entity acts as the lookup table that provides the values for the Value Help.

Below is the content of my schema.cds file.

namespace demo.valuehelp;

entity Products {
  key ID       : UUID;
  name         : String(100);
  category     : String(10);
  price        : Decimal(10,2);
}

entity Categories {
  key code     : String(10);
  description  : String(50);
}

Step 2 – Create Sample Data

Next, inside the db/data folder, I created two CSV files.

  • demo.valuehelp-Categories.csv
  • demo.valuehelp-Products.csv

The Categories CSV file contains the values that will be displayed in the Value Help dialog.

Below is the content of the Categories CSV file.

code,description
ELEC,Electronics
FURN,Furniture
BOOK,Books

The Products CSV file contains sample product records.

ID,name,category,price
3b5a1e2a-1111-4a1a-9a1a-000000000001,Laptop,ELEC,55000.00
3b5a1e2a-1111-4a1a-9a1a-000000000002,Office Chair,FURN,4500.00
3b5a1e2a-1111-4a1a-9a1a-000000000003,Notebook Set,BOOK,250.00

Step 3 – Create the Service

Next, create a service.cds file inside the srv folder.

In this file, I exposed both entities as projections so that they are available through OData services.

Below is the content of my service.cds file.

using demo.valuehelp as db from '../db/schema';

service CatalogService {
  entity Products   as projection on db.Products;
  entity Categories as projection on db.Categories;
}

Step 4 – Configure Value Help

After exposing the required entities in the service, I created a Fiori application inside the app folder by selecting New Project from Template in SAP Business Application Studio. I chose my local CAP application as the data source, and the project automatically generated the annotations.cds file.

In this file, I added the required UI annotations and configured the @Common.ValueList annotation for the Category field. The CollectionPath property points to the Categories entity, while LocalDataProperty and ValueListProperty map the selected value from the Value Help dialog back to the Product entity.

One thing I learned during this implementation is that even a small typo in an annotation or entity name can prevent the Value Help from working, while no clear error is shown in the UI. I found it helpful to verify the service metadata and ensure that all entity and property names matched exactly before troubleshooting further. This saved me a significant amount of debugging time.

Below is the content of my annotations.cds file.

using CatalogService as service from '../../srv/service';

annotate service.Products with @(
  UI.SelectionFields: [ category, name ],
  UI.LineItem: [
    { Value: name },
    { Value: category },
    { Value: price }
  ]
) {
  category @Common.ValueList : {
    Label          : 'Category',
    CollectionPath : 'Categories',
    Parameters     : [
      { $Type: 'Common.ValueListParameterInOut', LocalDataProperty: category, ValueListProperty: 'code' },
      { $Type: 'Common.ValueListParameterDisplayOnly', ValueListProperty: 'description' }
    ]
  };
};

Step 5 – Run the Application

After completing the above configuration, open the terminal and execute the following command.

cds watch

Once the application starts successfully, open the generated Fiori application.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 


here click on the URL which is having index.html
In this application I created value Help for a Selection field I mean a Search field.
Click On the Value Help Icon 
Here is the Image How My Value Help is looking

How Does It Work?

Let's quickly understand what happens behind the scenes.

  • The Categories entity stores all valid category values.
  • The Products entity contains the Category field.
  • The service.cds file exposes both entities.
  • The annotations.cds file connects the Product Category field with the Categories entity by using the @Common.ValueList annotation.
  • When the application runs, SAP Fiori automatically recognizes the annotation and displays the Value Help dialog.

Because of this configuration, users don't have to type category values manually, reducing data entry mistakes and improving consistency.

Conclusion

In this blog, we learned how to implement Value Help in SAP CAPM by using a lookup entity, service projections, and CDS annotations.

We created the required database entities, loaded sample data using CSV files, exposed the entities through a CAP service, and configured the @Common.ValueList annotation to display the Value Help dialog in the SAP Fiori application.

This approach is simple, reusable, and can be applied to many business scenarios such as Customers, Suppliers, Departments, Countries, Employees, or any other master data.

I hope this blog helps you understand how Value Help works in SAP CAPM.

Thank you for reading!

entry.title: Partner Learning Accelerator: SAP Certified - Backend Developer - SAP CAP
entry.content:
Preparing for a certification is more than passing an exam. It's about building confidence to apply your skills when customers need them most.

If you're working with SAP BTP and developing cloud-native extensions, the Partner Learning Accelerator: SAP Certified - Backend Developer - SAP Cloud Application Programming Model is built for you.

During this 3-hour live session, you'll review the certification topics that matter most. From modeling data with CDS and building CAP applications in Node.js or Java to integrating SAP services, implementing security, and creating Fiori elements-based UIs, you'll strengthen the knowledge that supports real customer projects.

Plus, you'll hear practical guidance that helps connect certification concepts with everyday development scenarios. That's often the difference between recognizing an answer and understanding why it's the right one.

If you've been following the Building Side-by-Side Extensions on SAP BTP learning journey, this is the perfect next step.
